critic_raw:
The retrospective results from hospital eye centers do not justify mobile-van deployment for three interconnected reasons.

First, verification bias in the labels: Ophthalmologist labels were assigned to patients who completed specialty exams following referral. The referral itself was triggered by prior clinical suspicion. Patients without prior suspicion who may have disease were never confirmed via specialist exam and are therefore absent from the positive class in training. The model's performance metrics are estimated on this biased sample. In the mobile screening context, where the model is the entry point for referral decisions rather than a secondary screen, the label bias directly undermines the claimed sensitivity.

Second, device and operator shift: The mobile program uses different cameras and nurses rather than ophthalmology-trained staff. The vendor's claim of protocol similarity is not equivalent to demonstrated image feature similarity. In retinal imaging, camera model, dilation status, field of view, and photographer skill all substantially affect image characteristics. A model trained on hospital specialist images may produce systematically different scores on van-acquired images.

Third, referral capacity and threshold analysis: The deployment context involves rationing scarce ophthalmology follow-up appointments. The evaluation reports aggregate test metrics, not threshold-specific referral burden and miss rates in the target lower-prevalence population. At lower prevalence, even a well-calibrated model requires a lower threshold to maintain sensitivity, which changes the referral volume. The claim that the model reduces referrals by one-third is derived from the hospital-center evaluation and may not translate to the mobile setting.
defender_raw: The retrospective results represent meaningful evidence that the model has discriminative ability. The QC safeguard for unreadable images is a strength of the deployment plan, not a weakness. Lower prevalence changes calibration needs but the core model can be recalibrated. The appropriate next step is a prospective mobile pilot rather than immediate full deployment, and the model's hospital-center performance justifies funding that pilot.
adjudication_raw: The critic identifies all three required issues accurately and completely. The defender concedes the path forward is a prospective pilot, which is the correct recommendation when the current evidence is insufficient. Critique wins: hospital-center backtest alone does not justify mobile-van deployment.
all_issues_raised: verification bias — labels from referred patients only
referral capacity tradeoff not analyzed at threshold level in target population

empirical_test.condition: Prospective mobile-van study with independent specialist adjudication on all sampled patients (not just those above threshold), measuring sensitivity at planned operating point and comparing manual-review escalation rates
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Sensitivity at planned threshold below prespecified floor, or high escalation rate substantially reduces referral capacity benefit
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model maintains sensitivity floor with reduced referrals and manageable escalation in mobile setting
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Performance adequate on readable images but escalation rate prevents net capacity benefit assessment
